1: The divine word-reception formula vayehi devar-YHWH elai lemor (wayĕhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾēlay lēmōr), “the word of YHWH came to me saying,” opens the extended allegory. The formula is Ezekiel's signature marker of prophetic authority; it occurs over ninety times in the book, establishing each oracle's divine origin.
2: ben-adam shtei nashim bnot em achat hayu (ben-ʾādām štê nāšîm bĕnôt ʾēm ʾaḥat hāyû): “Son of man, two women, daughters of one mother.” The allegory opens by establishing the shared origin of the two sisters. Ben-adam (son of man) is Ezekiel's distinctive address, occurring 93 times in the book — it simultaneously emphasizes human creaturely status before the divine glory and authorizes the prophet as YHWH's representative spokesman.
3: vatiznenah bemitsrayim bineurehem zanu sham mudeku shadeyhen ushad almunotehem innu (wattiznenâ bĕmiṣrayim binʿûrêhen zānû šām mudĕkû šĕdêhen ûšĕd ʿalmûnôtêhen ʿinnû): Zanah (זָנָה, to prostitute/commit harlotry) is the governing verb of the entire chapter; it appears over 20 times. The chapter uses the most explicit sexual language in the OT not for titillation but for theological shock — to convey the magnitude of covenant betrayal that idolatry and foreign political alliances represent. The Egypt-origin of the whoredom (v. 3, 8, 19, 27) establishes that Israel's covenant-disloyalty is not an aberration but a pattern rooted in pre-Sinai bondage.
4: Oholah hagdolah ve-Oholibah achoteha vatihyena li vateldna banim uvanot veshmotehem Shomron Oholah virushalayim Oholibah (ʾŎhŏlāh haggĕdôlāh wĕ-ʾŎhŏlîbāh ʾăḥôtāh): The names are themselves theological judgments. Oholah (ʾŏhŏlāh, “her own tent” or “tent of her own making”) = Samaria/northern kingdom: she built her own worship-center (Jeroboam's golden calves) rather than worshiping at YHWH's tent. Oholibah (ʾŏhŏlîbāh, “my tent is in her”) = Jerusalem/southern kingdom: YHWH's presence dwelt in her via the temple — making her betrayal all the more inexcusable. The name distinction encodes the entire theological verdict: Samaria invented her own worship; Jerusalem had the real thing and abandoned it.
5: vatizna Oholah tachat vateehav el-asher hashorim shekheney aliyah Ashur (wattizne ʾŎhŏlāh taḥtay wattĕʾehab ʾel-ʾăšer šĕkēnêhā ʾaššûr): Tachat (under me, while belonging to me) — the betrayal is intensified by the covenant bond: she played the harlot while still YHWH's. The Assyrian alliance is the referent: Samaria's political vassalage to Assyria (2 Kings 17) is read as covenant adultery. The prophets consistently interpret foreign political alliances as acts of whoredom — seeking security from human powers rather than from YHWH is a theological act of faithlessness.
6: levushey techelet pahot useganim bachuri chemah kulam parashim rokevey susim (lĕbûšê tĕkēlet pāḥôt ûsĕgānîm baḥûrê ḥemdā kullām pārāšîm rōkĕbê sûsîm): Techelet (תְּכֵלֶת, blue/violet dye) is the color of Assyrian official garments. The vividness of the description — handsome young men, mounted on horses, clothed in prestigious blue — conveys the seductive appeal of imperial power. Israel was drawn to the visible glory of foreign empires. The same blue is the color of the tabernacle coverings and the priestly garments; by using this color for the Assyrian warriors, YHWH indicates that what Israel sought from Assyria (glory, power, protection) she already possessed in him.
7: vatiten znuteha lahem mivchar bney Ashur kol uvechol asher ahavah betamey gillulehem nitma (wattittēn zĕnûtêhā ʿălêhem mibḥar bĕnê ʾaššûr kol wĕbĕkol ʾăšer āhĕbâ bĕkol-gillûlêhem niṭmāʾ): Gillulim (גִּלּוּלִים, idols) is Ezekiel's characteristic pejorative term for idols — likely derived from gelel (dung pellets) or gal (log/block). It appears 39 times in Ezekiel alone (out of 48 OT occurrences), more than in any other book. The scatological contempt of the term is deliberate: Ezekiel's prophetic strategy is to defamiliarize the idols, stripping them of their glamour by naming them excrement.
8: vezet znuteha lo azva me-al mitsrayim ki otah shachevu bineureha vehem daku et-shad almunoteha vayishpeku et-taznutehem aliyah (wĕʾet zĕnûtêhā mimiṣrayim lōʾ ʿāzābāh): The Egypt-connection is theologically central: the whoredom patterns were learned in slavery and never abandoned. Ezekiel's reading inverts the Exodus narrative — rather than Israel being freed from Egypt's influence at the Red Sea, Egypt's spiritual contamination persisted. This is his radical diagnosis: Israel did not bring only slaves out of Egypt; they brought idolatrous habits that would corrupt them for generations (cf. Ezek 20:7-8: even in Egypt they did not forsake the idols of Egypt).
9: lachen natatiyah beyad meahaveyha beyad bney Ashur asher ahavah aliyehem (lākēn nĕtattîhā bĕyad mĕʾahăbêhā bĕyad bĕnê ʾaššûr ʾăšer āhăbāh): Natan beyad (gave into the hand) is the covenant-abandonment formula — YHWH withdraws his protection and delivers into the hands of the one pursued. The theological logic is stark: Oholah/Samaria ran after Assyria for protection; YHWH gives her over to Assyria as her destroyer. The very partner she sought for security becomes the instrument of her judgment. This is the pattern Paul identifies in Romans 1:24-28: God gave them over to what they desired.
10: hem gallu ervata beneyha uvnoteyha laqachu ve-otah bacherev haragu vatehi shem lannashim veshfatim asu vah (hēmmāh gillû ʿerwātāh bānêhā ûbĕnôtêhā lāqāḥû wĕʾôtāh baḥereb hārāgû): Gillah ervah (uncovered nakedness) is the covenant-shame enacted in judgment — the reversal of YHWH's covering in Ezek 16:8. The Assyrian deportation of Samaria (722 BCE) is this “execution of judgment.” The note “she became an object of infamy among women” (shem lenashim) uses the female-shaming language of ancient Near Eastern culture — her dishonor is public and complete.
11: vatere achoteha Oholibah vatashchet tahavata mimeha veet-znuteyha mimiznut achoteha (wattarĕʾ ʾăḥôtāh ʾŎhŏlîbāh wattašḥēt taʾăwātāh mimmennâ wĕʾet-zĕnûtêhā mizzĕnût ʾăḥôtāh): Vatashchet (more corrupt) — Jerusalem's sin is worse than Samaria's because she witnessed Samaria's punishment and still persisted. The principle is escalation: knowing the outcome of another's covenant-breach and continuing anyway is more culpable than ignorance. This is the logic behind Jesus's “more tolerable for Sodom” pronouncements (Matt 10:15; 11:23-24).
12: el-bney Ashur ahavah pahot useganim qerovim levushey mikhlol parashim rokevey susim bachure chemad kulam (ʾel-bĕnê ʾaššûr āhăbāh pāḥôt ûsĕgānîm qĕrôbîm lĕbûšê miklōl): The description of Oholibah's infatuation with Assyrian officials precisely mirrors verses 5-6 about Oholah — showing that both sisters fell for the same visual spectacle. The repetition is artistic: Oholibah saw what happened to Oholah and was seduced by the same images anyway.
13: vaere ki nitma derekh echad lishtehem (wāʾerĕʾ kî niṭmāʾ dereḵ ʾeḥād lišĕtêhem): “I saw that she was defiled — both had taken the same way.” YHWH sees (vayare) and pronounces the verdict: tamei (defiled/unclean) — the technical cultic-holiness term applied to spiritual and moral corruption. Derekh echad (one way/path) — they converge in the same trajectory of apostasy. The watchman-YHWH sees what the nation refuses to see about itself.
14: vatosed el-znuteyha vatere anshey mechukeh al-haqqir tsalmey Kasdim mechukke veshashar (wattôsep ʾel-zĕnûtêhā wattarĕʾ anšê mĕḥuqqeh ʿal-haqqîr ṣalmê kaśdîm mĕḥuqqê bassāšar): Tsalmey Kasdim (images/portraits of Chaldeans) — painted murals of Babylonian warriors. Shashar (שָׁשַׁר, red ochre/vermilion) is the pigment used for decorative paintings. The sight of painted warriors triggers desire — a profound observation about the psychology of idolatry: the image (tselem) inflames the imagination. This is also a wordplay on the divine tselem (image/likeness) in Gen 1:26-27 — humanity, made in God's image, loses itself chasing counterfeit images.
15: azone ezor bematnehem saruchey tifara beresheyhem mareh shalishim kulam demet bney Bavel Kasdim erets moladatam (ʾăzûnê ʾēzôr bĕmotnêhem sĕrûḥê ṭifārâ bĕrāʾšêhem marʾēh šālîšîm kullām dĕmût bĕnê-bābel kaśdîm ʾereṣ môlādātām): The attention to fashion detail — belted waists, elaborate headdresses, officer-like appearance — is part of Ezekiel's satirical strategy. What Jerusalem found glamorous (Babylonian military fashion) is described with a cataloguer's precision that simultaneously deflates its mystique. Babylon is fully named here: eretz moladatam (the land of their birth) — this is not mysterious or divine; it is merely human geography.
16: vatahav aliyehem lemarat eneyha vatishlach malakhim aliyehem Kasdima (wattĕʾehab ʾălêhem lĕmirʾēh ʿênêhā wattišlaḥ malʾākîm ʾălêhem kaśdîmāh): Lemarat eneyha (at the sight of her eyes) — the immediacy of the desire, triggered by sight alone. Vatishlach malakhim (sent messengers to them) — the active pursuit reverses the expected gender dynamic. Jerusalem does not wait to be courted; she initiates. The theological point: covenant-breaking is not passive drift but active, deliberate pursuit of what YHWH has prohibited.
17: vayavo-u eliyah bney Bavel lemishtav dodim vayetamu-u otah betaznuteham vatatema bam vataqats naphsha mehem (wayyābōʾû ʾēlêhā bĕnê-bābel lĕmiškaḇ dōdîm wayyĕṭammĕʾû ʾôtāh bĕtaznûtām): Mishkab dodim (bed of love/beloved's bed) — the language inverts the covenantal “bed” of faithful union. The same root (dod) is used in the Song of Songs for legitimate conjugal love; here it is the language of covenant betrayal. The clause “her heart turned away from them” (vataqats naphsha mehem) is psychologically acute: the infatuation cools after the act, leaving only defilement.
18: vategel znuteyha vetegal et-ervatah vataqats naphshi mealiyah kasher naqtsah naphshi me-al achoteha (wattĕgal zĕnûtêhā wattĕgal ʾet-ʿerwātāh wattāqoṣ naphšî mēʿālêhā kaʾăšer nāqŏṣâ naphšî mēʿal ʾăḥôtāh): YHWH's response is expressed in anthropomorphic emotional language: vataqats naphshi (my soul/being turned in disgust). The same verb used for Jerusalem's own disgust at Babylon (v. 17) is now applied to YHWH's turning from Jerusalem — a precise reversal. The God who is repelled by covenant betrayal experiences the same revulsion the unfaithful wife felt for her paramour.
19: vatarbe et-znuteyha lizkor et-yemey neureha asher zantah be-erets mitsrayim (wattarbeh ʾet-zĕnûtêhā lizkōr ʾet-yĕmê nĕʿûrêhā ʾăšer zānĕtāh bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim): Lizkor (to remember/recall) — memory as spiritual danger. Ezekiel diagnoses the spiritual disease as nostalgic memory of Egypt: the very act of remembering the past generates renewed desire for what was. This is the psychology of recidivism — dwelling on former sins reinflames them. The spiritual discipline implied is the opposite: forgetting what lies behind (Phil 3:13) and not returning to the vomit (Prov 26:11; 2 Pet 2:22).
20: vatahev al-pilagsheyhem asher besar chamorim besaram uvezirmat susim zirmatam (wattĕʾehab ʿal-pîlgĕšêhem ʾăšer bĕśar ḥămôrîm bĕśārām ûzirmat sûsîm zimmātām): Zirmah (זִרְמָה, seminal emission) and basar (flesh) — Ezekiel uses the most anatomically explicit language in the OT to describe Jerusalem's spiritual condition. The graphic comparison to donkeys and horses is a deliberate desecration of the romantic narrative: what Jerusalem calls love is mere animal lust without covenant meaning. The theological purpose is total defamiliarization — to make the reader see foreign political/religious alliances as Ezekiel (and YHWH) see them: as debasement, not glamour.
21: vatiphqedi et-zimmat neureykhi badaqdeq shadayikh lemaan dade mitsrayim (wattipqĕdî ʾet-zimmat nĕʿûrāyik bĕdadqēq šādayik lĕmaʿan dĕdê miṣrāyim): Vatiphqedi (you called to mind/remembered — Hiphil) — the deliberate act of remembering compounds the guilt. Zimmat neureykhi (lewdness of your youth) — the sin of origins is being consciously revived. The Egypt-sexual imagery (breasts pressed, youthful nipples) creates a theological scandal: the founding redemptive narrative of the Exodus is contaminated at its source by Israel's spiritual harlotry.
22: lachen Oholibah ko amar Adonai YHWH hineni meir et-meahaveykhi alaykhi me-asher naqa naphsheykhi mehem veheveti otam alaykhi miqqol saviv (lākēn ʾŎhŏlîbāh kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh hinnênî mēʿîr ʾet-mĕʾahăbayik ʿālayik): Hineni meir (I am raising up) — the divine first-person announcement of judgment. The ironic justice: YHWH will use the very partners Jerusalem pursued (Babylonians, Assyrians) as the instruments of her punishment. The lovers become the executioners. This is the classic prophetic judgment-pattern: the means of covenant-breaking become the means of covenant-curse.
23: Bavel vekhol Kasdim Pekod veShoa veQoa vekhol bney Ashur itam bachure chemad kulam pahot useganim sarshim veqruim kulam parashim (bābel wĕkhol kaśdîm pĕqōd wĕšôaʿ wĕqôaʿ wĕkol bĕnê ʾaššûr): The catalog of nations — Pekod, Shoa, and Koa — are Aramean/Babylonian tribal confederates known from Akkadian records. The specificity of the list is polemical: these are real historical peoples, not mythological forces. YHWH's judgment comes through concrete geopolitical realities, not mystical interventions.
24: uvau alaykhi rechev karkar vegalgal uveqahal amim tsina vemagen veqova yassimu alaykhi saviv venatati lifneihem mishpat ushefatu otakh bemishpeteyhem (ûbāʾû ʿālayik reḵeb karkār wĕgalgal ûbeqahal ʿammîm): Mishpat (judgment, ordinances) — “they will judge you by their own ordinances” is a chilling phrase: the Babylonian legal system, not the Torah, will be the instrument of Jerusalem's judgment. This is the covenantal irony: the nation that abandoned YHWH's mishpat will be judged by pagan mishpat. The loss of covenant-protection means vulnerability to foreign legal systems.
25: venatati kinat bi veashu otakh bechema af veoznekh veoznekh yikratu vesheritek bacherev tipol beney uvnotekh yiqachu vacharitekh baesh tikle (wĕnāṭattî qinʾātî bāk wĕʿāśû ʾôtāk bĕḥēmā): Qinah (קִנְאָה, jealousy/zeal) — YHWH's jealousy is the covenant emotion of the marriage bond. The physical mutilation described (cutting off nose and ears) is attested in ancient Near Eastern punishment for adultery. The nose-ring and ear-rings were symbols of the covenant adorning (Ezek 16:11-12); their removal is the covenant-curse enacted on the body.
26: hikhu begdeykhi velaqchu kley tipherteykhi (hikkû bigdāyik wĕlāqĕḥû kĕlê tipʾarteykhi): Bigdayikh (your garments) and kley tipherteykhi (your beautiful ornaments) — these are the garments and adornments YHWH gave Jerusalem in the covenant-marriage (Ezek 16:10-13): fine linen, silk, embroidered cloth, bracelets, necklace, nose-ring, earrings, crown. Their stripping reverses the covenant-clothing. Exile is unclothing — the removal of the divine gifts that marked covenant status.
27: vehishabati zimmatekhi mimeykh veznut heveyt me-erets mitsrayim velo tise-i einayikh alayehem ulomitsrayim lo tizkeri-od (wĕhišbattî zimmatēk mimmēk wĕzĕnût hûbāʾt mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim wĕlōʾ tiśśĕʾî ʿênayik ʾălêhem): Vehishabati zimmatekhi (I will bring your lewdness to an end) — the judicial resolution formula. The prophecy points toward a post-judgment purification in which the Egypt-pattern that has dominated all of Israel's history will finally be broken. The “no longer looking toward Egypt” anticipates the new covenant transformation (Ezek 36:24-28) when YHWH himself enables what Israel could not accomplish through her own moral effort.
28: ki ko amar Adonai YHWH hineni noten otach beyad sane-aykh beyad asher qatsa naphsheykh mehem (kî kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh hinnênî nōtēn ʾôtāk bĕyad śōnĕʾayik bĕyad ʾăšer qāṣâ naphšēk mēhem): The divine messenger formula (ko amar Adonai YHWH) marks this as the authoritative royal decree. “Into the hand of those from whom your heart has turned” — the handing-over is precisely to the ones Jerusalem abandoned in disgust. The judgment is a divinely ordered inversion of desire: the ones rejected become the ones who dominate.
29: veashu vekhi binah velachu yegiakhi vehenichu otakh eirom vearum venikla ervat znutaykhi vezimmatekh venaphutaykhi (wĕʿāśû ʾôtāk bĕśinʾāh wĕlāqĕḥû kol yĕgîʿēk wĕhinnîḥû ʾôtāk ʿêrōm wĕʿeryāh): Einah (hatred) — the lovers' hatred completes the trajectory: desire → defilement → disgust → hatred. The progression describes the dynamics of idolatrous attachment: the thing pursued with such intensity ultimately generates contempt in both directions. Eirom vearum (naked and bare) — the covenant-clothing stripped away, returning Jerusalem to the exposed infant state of Ezek 16:4.
30: asitah eleh timaeti ki zint achrey hagoyim al gillulehem nitme-it (ʿāśtāh ʾēlleh ʾittāk bĕzanôtēk ʾaḥărê haggôyim ʿal gillûlêhem niṭmēʾt): Titme-i (you defiled yourself) — the reflexive form emphasizes personal agency. Gillulim again — the contamination of idolatry is not merely ritualistic but ontological; contact with “dung-pellets” makes one “dung.” The nations' idols are called gillulim precisely because Israel became like what she worshiped (Ps 115:8: “those who make them become like them”).
31: bederekh achoteykhi halachti velaqachti kosah beyadeykh (bĕdereḵ ʾăḥôtēk hālaket wĕnātatî kôsāh bĕyādēk): Kos achoteykhi (your sister's cup) — the cup of judgment is a major prophetic image (Jer 25:15-17; Isa 51:17-23; Rev 14:10; 16:19). Walking in the way of Oholah/Samaria means inheriting Samaria's cup. The cup-image captures both the communal nature of judgment (a cup is passed around) and its intoxicating, destabilizing quality — judgment comes as a force that overwhelms self-control.
32: ko amar Adonai YHWH kos achoteykhi tisht-i hammuqah veharehivah tehiyeh litshoq velalas harbe lehachil (kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh kôs ʾăḥôtēk tištî haʿamuqâ wĕhārĕḥābāh tihyeh liṣḥōq wĕlālaʿag harbēh lĕhāḵîl): The cup of judgment is described as amuqah verehivah (deep and wide) — its capacity exceeds what can be drunk at once. The mockery (tshoq velaalas) that accompanies the drinking adds shame to suffering. In Revelation's eschatology (Rev 14:10; 16:19), the cup of God's wrath is the final consummation of this Ezekielian cup-of-judgment theme.
33: shikkaron veyagon timale-i kos shomah ushemamah kos achoteykhi Shomron (šikkārôn wĕyāgôn timmālĕʾî kôs šammāh ûšĕmāmāh kôs ʾăḥôtēk šōmĕrôn): Shikkaron (שִׁכָּרוֹן, drunkenness) and yagon (יָגוֹן, grief/sorrow) — the cup produces both intoxication (loss of capacity for rational self-defense) and anguish. Shomah ushemamah (horror and desolation) — the cup is Samaria's own experience of 722 BCE. Jerusalem is about to drink what her sister drank.
34: ushtit vematsit vecherasheyha tilchakhi veeth sadeykhi tinaqeri ki ani dibarti neum Adonai YHWH (ûšātît wĕmāṣît wĕḥarāśêhā tillaḥăkî wĕʾet šādayik tĕnaqqērî kî ʾănî dibbartî nĕʾum ʾădōnāy Yhwh): Ushtit umatsit (drink and drain it) — the cup must be completely emptied; there is no setting it down half-finished. The self-mutilation (gnawing the shards, tearing out the breasts) conveys the total self-destruction that comes when judgment runs its full course. The closing formula neum Adonai YHWH (declares the Lord GOD) is Ezekiel's divine-signature stamp: this is not poetic hyperbole but divine decree.
35: lachen ko amar Adonai YHWH yaan shachacht oti vatashlikhiy achoreyikh et-guve vasgay zimmatekhi veznutekhi (lākēn kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿan šākaḥt ʾôtî wattašlîḵî ʾaḥărêk ʾet-gēwek): Shachacht oti (you forgot me) — the covenant-breach is defined as forgetting. The opposite of faithful covenant-love is not hatred but forgetting — treating YHWH as irrelevant to daily political and religious life. Vatashlikhiy achoreyikh (cast behind your back) — the active dismissal of YHWH into irrelevance. This is the theological diagnosis beneath all the allegory: structural amnesia toward the covenant God.
36: vayomer YHWH elai ben-adam hashpot hashpot et Oholah ve-et-Oholibah vehagid lahem et-toavoteyhem (wayyōmer Yhwh ʾēlay ben-ʾādām hăšpôṭ hăšpōṭ ʾet-ʾŎhŏlāh wĕʾet-ʾŎhŏlîbāh wĕhaggēd lāhem ʾet-tôʿăbōtêhem): Hashpot hashpot (will you judge / judge!) — the doubled imperative (infinitive absolute + finite verb) emphasizes the urgency and authority of the prophetic indictment. The prophet as judge (shofet) is acting in YHWH's court, presenting evidence. The declaration of toavot (abominations) is not personal vituperation but legal testimony before the divine tribunal.
37: ki niefufah vi veyad dam lifneyhen ihe veet-gillulehem niefufah vatehayinah baney asher yeladu li heviru lahem baesh leakhela (kî niʾăp̄û wĕyādê dāmîm lifnêhen ûbĕgillûlêhem niʾāp̄û wĕʾet-bĕnêhem ʾăšer yālĕdû-lî hiʿbîrû lāhem bāʾēš lĕʾāklâ): Niafufah vi (they committed adultery with/against me) — adultery against YHWH and idolatry are synonymous. Heeviru baesh (pass through fire) is Molech worship — child sacrifice to the Ammonite deity. “Children they bore to me” — these children were covenant members, belonging to YHWH, sacrificed to idols. The covenantal crime intensifies: not their own lives but their children's lives are sacrificed to gillulim.
38: od zot asu li timeu et-miqdashi vayom hahu veet-shabbetotay chilelu (ʿôd zōʾt ʿāśû lî ṭimmĕʾû ʾet-miqdāšî bayyôm hahûʾ wĕʾet-šabbĕtôtay ḥillēlû): Timeu miqdashi (defiled my sanctuary) — the sanctuary-defilement motif is central to Ezekiel's theology. The very space where YHWH's presence dwells becomes the site of abomination when covenant-people bring their defiled selves and objects into it. “On the same day” — the temple and Molech worship happening simultaneously, perhaps on the Sabbath itself, is the maximum possible sacrilege.
39: ki veshochtam et-beneihem legilulehem vayavo-u el-miqdashi vayom hahu lechallelo vehine chen asu betokh beiti (kî bĕšoḥăṭām ʾet-bĕnêhem lĕgillûlêhem wayyābōʾû ʾel-miqdāšî bayyôm hahûʾ lĕḥallĕlô): The sequence — slaughter children for idols, then enter the sanctuary — reveals the complete collapse of the distinction between sacred and profane. The people move between Molech-worship and YHWH-worship as though both are equally valid religious options. Ezekiel's horror is theological: the sanctuary becomes complicit in the abomination when those who have just sacrificed children to idols enter it unrebuked.
40: vaaf ki tishlechna el-anashim bai-im memarchaq asher malach shalach alayhem vehine bau asher rachatzta kachalt einayikh veatdiy adi (wĕʾap̄ kî tišlaḥnâ ʾel-ʾănāšîm bāʾîm mimmereḥōq ʾăšer malʾāk šālaḥ ʾălêhem): Kachalt einayikh (painted your eyes) — cosmetic eye-painting, widely practiced in the ancient Near East, was associated with seduction. The same verb (puk) is used for Jezebel's eye-painting before Jehu's arrival (2 Kings 9:30) and for the harlot's preparations (Prov 7:17). Jerusalem is the seducer who adorns herself to attract foreign nations — reversing the expected direction of political dependency.
41: veyashavt al-mitat mechubad veshulchan arukh lefaneyha veqotereti veshemeni samta aleyha (wĕyāšabt ʿal-miṭṭat mĕkubbād wĕšulḥān ʿārûk lĕpānêhā wĕqoṭartî ûšamnî śamtĕ ʿālêhā): Qotereti veshemeni (my incense and my oil) — YHWH's covenant gifts (incense and oil designated for the tabernacle service) are placed on a table prepared for foreign guests. The sacred materials of YHWH-worship are repurposed for covenant-breaking hospitality. This is the logic of spiritual syncretism: taking what belongs to YHWH and putting it in service of other loyalties.
42: veqol hamon shalem bo avel el-anashim merov adam muveim midbara vayitnu tsemidim al-yadeyhen vetiferet atarot al-rosheyhen (weqôl hāmôn šālēw bāh wĕʾel-ʾănāšîm mērōb ʾādām mûbāʾîm midbārâ wayyittĕnû ṣemîdîm ʿal-yĕdêhen): Midbar (wilderness) — the Sabeans come from the wilderness. The bracelets (tsemidim) and crowns (atarot) placed on these women by foreign guests are debased versions of the covenant adornments YHWH gave (Ezek 16:11-12). Foreign gifts replace divine gifts as markers of identity.
43: vaomar lablah niufim ata yiznu znuteha vehi (wāʾōmar lablāh nîʾupîm ʿattāh yizněnû zĕnûtêhā wĕhîʾ): Lablah niufim (worn out/exhausted by adulteries) — YHWH's rhetorical question has bitter irony: will they still go to her? — even now, when she is worn out? The question implies the hopelessness of the situation: even the withered, exhausted idolater still attracts her idolatrous partners. Sin has its own momentum that persists beyond reason or dignity.
44: ubau-u eleyha kasher yavo-u el-isha zonah ken bau el-Oholah ve-el-Oholibah nashim hazimot (ûbāʾû ʾēlêhā kaʾăšer yābōʾû ʾel-ʾiššâ zônāh kēn bāʾû ʾel-ʾŎhŏlāh wĕʾel-ʾŎhŏlîbāh nāšîm hazzimmôt): Nashim hazimot (lewd/scheming women) — zimmah (זִמָּה) means both lewdness and deliberate scheming. The term appears in Leviticus for the most serious sexual crimes. Both sisters are now formally named zimmot — a legal-theological verdict. The allegory arrives at its judicial climax: they are not merely flawed but categorically criminal by Torah standards.
45: anashim tsadiqim hema yishpetu otam mishpat noaphot umishpat shofakhot dam ki noaphot henah vedam biyadeyhem (ʾănāšîm ṣaddîqîm hēmmāh yišpĕṭû ʾôtām mišpaṭ nōʾăp̄ôt ûmišpaṭ šōp̄ĕkôt dām kî nōʾăp̄ôt hēnnâ wĕdām bĕyĕdêhen): Anshe tsadiqim (righteous men) — the righteous community as co-judges reflects the covenant assembly's role in executing Torah-judgment. The dual charges are adultery (niufim) and bloodshed (shofakhot dam) — the two great covenant-crimes that defile the land (Num 35:33-34). Together they represent the collapse of the two great commandments: faithfulness to God (adultery against YHWH) and protection of human life (bloodshed).
46: ki ko amar Adonai YHWH haaleh aleyhen qahal venaten otahan lerogzah velabaz (kî kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh haʿălēh ʾălêhen qāhāl wĕnātan ʾôtāhen lĕrāgzāh wĕlābaz): Qahal (assembly/congregation) brought against them — the same word used for the covenant assembly of Israel (qahal YHWH) is now weaponized: the assembly assembles not for worship but for execution of judgment. The covenant community that should have been a worshiping congregation becomes instead the instrument of covenantal punishment.
47: veragmu alayhen even qahal vebareh othan becharbot baneyhen uvnoteyhen yaharegu uvateyhenn beesh yisrofu (wĕrāgĕmû ʾălêhen ʾeben qāhāl ûbāreʾ ʾôtāhen beḥarbôt): Ragam even (stoning with stones) — the covenant penalty for adultery (Deut 22:21-24; John 8:5). Burning of houses compounds the judgment. The execution of the allegorical sisters is the prophetic coding of the Babylonian destruction: the armies are the assembly, the sword is the execution, the burning is the 586 BCE conflagration. The allegory provides the theological meaning for what would otherwise be mere military history.
48: vehishabati zimma meen-haaretz veniyssiru kol-hanashim velo taasena kezimmatkhena (wĕhišbattî zimmāh mēhāʾāreṣ wĕniwwasrû kol-hannāšîm wĕlōʾ taʿăśenāh kezimmatĕkennāh): Vehishabati zimma meen-haaretz (I will bring lewdness to an end from the land) — the judgment has a restorative purpose: to remove the pattern of covenant betrayal from the land so that the land itself can be healed. Veniyssiru kol-hanashim (all women may be warned/instructed) — the allegory serves a pedagogical function for future generations: the account of Oholah and Oholibah is a warning, not merely a condemnation.
49: venasenu zimmatkhena alaykhena vechet gilluleykhen tise-na vidu-aten ki ani Adonai YHWH (wĕnāśʾû zimmatkennāh ʿălêkennāh wĕḥêṭ gillûlêkhen tiśśeʾnāh wîdaʿten kî ʾănî ʾădōnāy Yhwh): Nasenu zimmatkena alaykhena (your lewdness returned upon you) — the covenant-curse principle of Leviticus 26 in operation: the sin comes back upon the sinner. Yadatem ki ani Adonai YHWH — the recognition formula that closes the oracle. The purpose of judgment is not mere punishment but knowledge: when the judgment falls, Israel will at last know who YHWH truly is — not the abstracted deity they treated as one option among many, but the sole sovereign LORD who keeps covenant and keeps judgment.
